channel.title: 基于CUDA的SPH代码算例 on Keqi的博客
channel.description: Recent content in 基于CUDA的SPH代码算例 on Keqi的博客

item.title: 1D Sod Shock Tube Experiment - CUDA SPH Benchmark
item.author: plloningye@gmail.com (Keqi Ye)
item.description:
1D Sod Shock Tube Experiment

TL;DR

This article presents a reproduction of the classic 1D Sod Shock Tube benchmark test using a custom-built CUDA SPH (Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics) physics engine from scratch. The primary objective is to verify the engine’s capability to capture strong shock discontinuities. A critical numerical pitfall was identified and resolved: when handling initial step density fields, the traditional Continuity Density method causes severe non-physical oscillations, while the direct Summation Density method demonstrates excellent numerical robustness.

1. Visual Impact

Comparison of two density calculation methods:

Summation Density (Success)

Continuity Density (Failure)

  • Left (Success): Using summation density, the shock front, contact discontinuity, and rarefaction wave can be clearly observed propagating smoothly.
  • Right (Failure): Using continuity density, severe interface instability and chaotic particle distribution appear at the initial density jump in the shock tube.

2. Problem Background

2.1 What is the Sod Shock Tube?

The Sod shock tube is a classic benchmark problem in fluid dynamics, used to verify numerical methods’ capability to capture shocks, contact discontinuities, and rarefaction waves. This problem simulates the instantaneous release of two gases at different states separated by a membrane in a one-dimensional tube.

2.2 Physical Model

This simulation uses the ideal gas model with the following governing equations:

Continuity Equation:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$$

Momentum Equation:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \mathbf{v})}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v} \otimes \mathbf{v}) = -\nabla p$$

Energy Equation:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho e)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho e \mathbf{v}) = -p \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}$$

Equation of State (Ideal Gas):

$$p = (\gamma - 1) \rho e$$

where $\gamma = 5/3$ is the adiabatic index.

3. Initial Conditions

At the initial time, the shock tube is divided into two regions by a membrane at $x=0$:

Parameter High Density Region (Left) Low Density Region (Right)
Density $\rho$ 1.0 0.25
Velocity $v$ 0.0 0.0
Internal Energy $e$ 2.5 1.795
Particle Spacing $\Delta x$ 0.001875 0.0075
Smoothing Length $h$ 0.01 0.01
Region Range $[-1.2, 0)$ $(0, 1.2]$
Number of Particles 640 160

Total Particles: 800

The initial conditions set the left side as a high-pressure, high-density region and the right side as a low-pressure, low-density region. After the membrane is removed, the high-pressure gas on the left accelerates to the right, forming three wave structures: shock, contact discontinuity, and rarefaction wave.

4. Numerical Methods

4.1 SPH Density Calculation Approaches

This benchmark compares two SPH density computation methods:

Method 1: Summation Density

$$\rho_i = \sum_j m_j W(r_{ij}, h)$$

where $W(r, h)$ is the kernel function and $h$ is the smoothing length. This method directly sums the masses of neighboring particles with clear physical meaning.

Method 2: Continuity Density

$$\frac{d\rho_i}{dt} = \sum_j m_j (v_j - v_i) \cdot \nabla_i W(r_{ij}, h)$$

This equation is derived from the divergence of the momentum equation and is theoretically equivalent to the continuity equation.

4.2 Why Does Continuity Density Fail?

At the initial time, the shock tube has a step density field (left: $\rho=1.0$, right: $\rho=0.25$). The continuity density equation relies on the computation of velocity divergence:

  • At the density jump, velocity divergence requires precise capture of tiny variations
  • At the initial moment, all particle velocities are zero, causing $v_j - v_i = 0$
  • Numerical errors are amplified, generating non-physical density perturbations at the interface
  • These perturbations further affect pressure computation, creating a vicious cycle

In contrast, the summation density method directly computes density without depending on the velocity field, thus exhibiting excellent numerical robustness when handling initial step density fields.

4.3 Kernel Function

This simulation uses the Cubic Spline Kernel:

$$W(r, h) = \frac{1}{h^d} \begin{cases} 1 - \frac{3}{2}q^2 + \frac{3}{4}q^3 & 0 \leq q < 1 \\ \frac{1}{4}(2 - q)^3 & 1 \leq q < 2 \\ 0 & q \geq 2 \end{cases}$$

where $q = r/h$ and $d$ is the spatial dimension.

5. Results Analysis

5.1 Comparison with Analytical Solution

Figure 1: Density distribution comparison for the Sod shock tube. Shows the density along the x-direction at $t=0.2$ for three methods compared with the analytical solution. The red solid line is the analytical solution, blue dots are numerical results from the summation density method, and orange dots are numerical results from the continuity density method.

As clearly shown in the figure: the summation density method’s results closely match the analytical solution, successfully capturing the sharp jump at the shock front, the density plateau at the contact discontinuity, and the smooth transition in the rarefaction wave region. The continuity density method produces severe non-physical oscillations at the interface, completely failing to correctly simulate the flow structure.

5.2 Wave Structure Identification

Three typical wave structures form in the shock tube problem:

  1. Shock Front: A discontinuity propagating to the right with abrupt changes in velocity and density
  2. Contact Discontinuity: A density jump with continuous pressure, propagating to the right following the shock
  3. Rarefaction Wave: An expansion wave propagating to the left with smooth decreases in velocity and density

6. Conclusion

This article validates the core algorithms of the custom CUDA SPH engine through the 1D Sod shock tube benchmark. Key findings:

  1. Summation Density exhibits significant advantages when handling initial step density fields, avoiding the numerical instability of the continuity density equation
  2. The engine successfully captures all three wave structures: shock, contact discontinuity, and rarefaction wave
  3. The numerical solution closely matches the analytical solution

Future Work

  • 2D Cylinder Flow Simulation
  • 3D Dam Break Simulation
  • Multi-phase Flow Interface Stability Analysis

References

  1. Sod, G. A. (1978). A survey of several finite difference methods for systems of nonlinear hyperbolic conservation laws. Journal of Computational Physics, 27(1), 1-31.
  2. Monaghan, J. J. (1992). Smoothed particle hydrodynamics. Annual Review of Astronomy and Astrophysics, 30, 543-574.
  3. Liu, G. R., & Liu, M. B. (2003). Smoothed particle hydrodynamics: a meshfree particle method. World Scientific.

item.title: 1D Sod 激波管实验 - CUDA SPH 基准测试
item.author: plloningye@gmail.com (Keqi Ye)
item.description:
1D Sod 激波管实验

TL;DR

本文使用从零自研的 CUDA SPH 物理引擎复现了经典的 1D Sod 激波管 (Shock Tube) 基准测试，主要验证引擎对强激波不连续面的捕捉能力。测试中解决了一个关键的数值陷阱：在处理初始阶跃密度场时，传统的连续性方程 (Continuity Density) 会导致严重的非物理震荡，而直接求和密度 (Summation Density) 展现出了极强的数值鲁棒性。

1. Visual Impact

以下是两种密度计算方法的效果对比：

求和密度法 (成功)

连续密度法 (失败)

  • 左图 (成功)：采用求和密度法，可以清晰看到激波波前 (Shock front)、接触间断 (Contact discontinuity) 和稀疏波 (Rarefaction wave) 的光滑推进。
  • 右图 (失败)：采用连续密度法，在激波管的初始密度阶跃处产生了严重的界面不稳定性和杂乱的粒子分布。

2. 问题背景

2.1 什么是 Sod 激波管？

Sod 激波管是流体力学中经典的基准测试问题，用于验证数值方法对激波、接触间断和稀疏波的捕捉能力。该问题模拟了一维管道中由薄膜分隔的两种不同状态气体的瞬时释放过程。

2.2 物理模型

本模拟采用理想气体模型，方程组包括：

连续性方程：

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$$

动量方程：

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \mathbf{v})}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v} \otimes \mathbf{v}) = -\nabla p$$

能量方程：

$$\frac{\partial (\rho e)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho e \mathbf{v}) = -p \nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}$$

状态方程（理想气体）：

$$p = (\gamma - 1) \rho e$$

其中 $\gamma = 5/3$ 为绝热指数。

3. 初始条件

初始时刻，激波管被位于 $x=0$ 的薄膜分为左右两个区域：

参数 高密度区 (左) 低密度区 (右)
密度 $\rho$ 1.0 0.25
速度 $v$ 0.0 0.0
内能 $e$ 2.5 1.795
粒子间距 $\Delta x$ 0.001875 0.0075
软化长度 $h$ 0.01 0.01
区域范围 $[-1.2, 0)$ $(0, 1.2]$
粒子数 640 160

总粒子数：800 个

初始条件设置使得左侧为高压高密度区，右侧为低压低密度区。薄膜移除后，左侧的高压气体会向右加速，形成激波、接触间断和稀疏波三种波结构。

4. 数值方法

4.1 SPH 密度计算方式

本测试对比了两种 SPH 密度计算方法：

方法一：求和密度 (Summation Density)

$$\rho_i = \sum_j m_j W(r_{ij}, h)$$

其中 $W(r, h)$ 为核函数，$h$ 为软化长度。这种方法直接对邻域粒子的质量进行求和，物理意义明确。

方法二：连续密度 (Continuity Density)

$$\frac{d\rho_i}{dt} = \sum_j m_j (v_j - v_i) \cdot \nabla_i W(r_{ij}, h)$$

该方程通过对动量方程进行散度运算得到，理论上与连续性方程等价。

4.2 为什么连续密度法失效？

在初始时刻，激波管存在一个阶跃密度场（左侧 $\rho=1.0$，右侧 $\rho=0.25$）。连续密度方程依赖于速度散度的计算：

  • 在密度阶跃处，速度散度需要精确捕捉微小变化
  • 初始时刻所有粒子速度为零，导致 $v_j - v_i = 0$
  • 数值误差被放大，在界面处产生非物理的密度扰动
  • 这些扰动进一步影响压力计算，形成恶性循环

相比之下，求和密度法直接计算密度，不依赖于速度场，因此在处理初始阶跃密度场时具有极强的数值鲁棒性。

4.3 核函数

本模拟采用三次样条核函数 (Cubic Spline Kernel)：

$$W(r, h) = \frac{1}{h^d} \begin{cases} 1 - \frac{3}{2}q^2 + \frac{3}{4}q^3 & 0 \leq q < 1 \\ \frac{1}{4}(2 - q)^3 & 1 \leq q < 2 \\ 0 & q \geq 2 \end{cases}$$

其中 $q = r/h$，$d$ 为空间维度。

5. 结果分析

5.1 与解析解对比

图 1：Sod 激波管密度分布对比。展示了三种方法在 $t=0.2$ 时刻的密度沿 $x$ 方向分布与解析解的对比。红色实线为解析解，蓝色点为求和密度法的数值结果，橙色点为连续密度法的数值结果。

从图中可以清晰看到：求和密度法的结果与解析解高度吻合，成功捕捉到了激波波前的尖锐跳跃、接触间断处的密度平台，以及稀疏波区域的平滑过渡。而连续密度法在界面处产生了严重的非物理震荡，完全无法正确模拟流动结构。

5.2 波结构识别

激波管问题会形成三种典型的波结构：

  1. 激波 (Shock Front)：向右传播的间断面，速度和密度发生突变
  2. 接触间断 (Contact Discontinuity)：密度发生跳跃但压力连续，跟随激波向右传播
  3. 稀疏波 (Rarefaction Wave)：向左传播的膨胀波，速度和密度平滑下降

6. 总结

本文通过 1D Sod 激波管基准测试，验证了自研 CUDA SPH 引擎的核心算法。关键发现：

  1. 求和密度法在处理初始阶跃密度场时具有显著优势，避免了连续密度方程的数值不稳定性
  2. 引擎成功捕捉到了激波、接触间断和稀疏波三种波结构
  3. 数值解与解析解吻合良好

后续计划

  • 2D 圆柱绕流模拟
  • 3D 溃坝模拟
  • 多相流界面稳定性分析

参考资料

  1. Sod, G. A. (1978). A survey of several finite difference methods for systems of nonlinear hyperbolic conservation laws. Journal of Computational Physics, 27(1), 1-31.
  2. Monaghan, J. J. (1992). Smoothed particle hydrodynamics. Annual Review of Astronomy and Astrophysics, 30, 543-574.
  3. Liu, G. R., & Liu, M. B. (2003). Smoothed particle hydrodynamics: a meshfree particle method. World Scientific.
